text: We present a case of large renal arteriovenous malformation which was repaired with the help of robotic surgery.
A thirty years female with no medical risk factors presented with right flank pain of five years duration.
Her ultrasound revealed a four centimeter cystic lesion in the right kidney.

on ct scan there was a five point seven into three point six centimeter lesion at interpolar region with contrast filling in the arterial phase there was communication with the right renal vein and a tortuous right renal artery was present on angiography the sac was seen arising from the anterior division of renal artery
The global GFR was seventy seven ml per minute with right kidney contributing forty one ml per minute.
The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the ascending colon, likely using surgical instruments to carefully separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs caucarization of the duodenum, carefully manipulating the tissue to expose the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon mobilizes the lower pole of the right kidney, manipulating the surrounding tissue to improve access and visibility.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein as it enters medially into the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon ligates and cuts the gonadal vein to improve maneuverability around the arteriovenous malformation (AVM) sac.
The surgeon performs dissection along the lateral border of the inferior vena cava (IVC) to access the renal hilum, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon identifies the anterior division of the right renal artery as the first vessel, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the posterior division of the right renal artery, which is seen accompanying a branch of the renal vein.
The surgeon traces the anterior and posterior divisions to visualize the main trunk of the right renal artery, highlighting its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon loops the right renal vein, preparing it for further manipulation or ligation.
The surgeon loops the anterior division of the right renal artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon loops the main trunk of the right renal artery, ensuring full vascular control over the vessel.
The surgeon tightens the loop around the main trunk of the right renal artery, causing the sac to visibly deflate, and then it refills upon relaxation of the loop.
The surgeon tightens the loop around the anterior division of the right renal artery, causing visible deflation of the sac.
The surgeon dissects the sac circumferentially to expose the ostium, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The ostium is visually identified on the anterior division of the right renal artery, consistent with preoperative angiography findings.
Two small aneurysms are visible near the site of the ostia on the anterior division of the renal artery.

A vascular clamp is applied to the anterior division of the renal artery for vascular control. The surgeon identifies the single arterial feeder to the arteriovenous malformation (AVM), clips it, and cuts it to achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon freshens and repairs the margins of the ostia using a Gore-Tex suture, ensuring precise closure of the vascular opening.
Following the closure of the ostea, the surgeon removes the vascular clamp from the anterior division of the renal artery to assess hemostasis, ensuring no bleeding is visible.
The surgeon dissects the sac from the nearby parenchyma tissue, during which bleeding is observed from the surgical site.

The surgeon applies a vascular clamp on the main arterial trunk, observing the sac draining into a branch of the renal vein. The branch is then ligated and cut, followed by the removal of the vascular clamp from the main arterial trunk.
The surgical field is irrigated, and hemostasis is secured, ensuring no active bleeding is visible.
The surgeon performs renorrhaphy using 3-0 V-Loc sutures, applying two layers to close the renal tissue.
The surgeon identifies small aneurysms near the repaired ostea and reinforces them with vascular control to prevent further bleeding.
The surgeon reapplies vascular clamps both proximally and distally to control bleeding, then reinforces the aneurysm using a pledget sutured with Gore-Tex suture.
The surgeon removes the clamps and slings, checks hemostasis, applies FlowSeal to the parenchyma, and sutures a layer of fat over the kidney.
The surgeon presents the final specimen, a cystic lesion measuring approximately 4.5 centimeters in size.
The biopsy report came out as renal artery aneurysm.
The patient is planned for a three months follow up with CT angio.
In conclusion, we would like to say that surgical treatment is most likely to cure an AVM, especially in a symptomatic female of childbearing age.

usually in smaller avm options of embolization and conservative management is there but repeated treatments may be required a minimally invasive approach with robotic surgery and ligation of feeding vessels will help in decreasing the morbidity in complex avms a bench surgery with auto transplantation could be an option thank you
